name: RCU — Read-Copy-Update
summary: Paul McKenney's RCU (Linux 2002): readers execute lightweight critical sections; writers publish then wait a grace period — interval after which every CPU has passed a quiescent state — before reclaiming. Zero-overhead readers in non-preemptible kernels; hierarchical Tree RCU scales to thousands of CPUs. Foundational technique in the Linux kernel; userspace via liburcu; standardised as C++26 std::rcu (P2545).

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Paul McKenney's **RCU** (Linux 2002) is the canonical reader-friendly reclamation scheme: readers execute lightweight critical sections; writers publish then wait a *grace period* — an interval after which every CPU has passed a *quiescent state* — before reclaiming. The reader side has zero overhead in non-preemptible kernels (compiler barrier only, no atomics); the writer side waits for grace-period detection.

Hierarchical Tree RCU enables scalable grace-period detection across thousands of CPUs. The `rcu_assign_pointer` / `rcu_dereference` publish-subscribe pattern doubles as a memory-ordering API, so RCU is simultaneously a reclamation mechanism and a publication discipline.

sections.title: Cost model and trade-offs
sections.content:
Cost: reader 0–2 instructions (preempt-disable in classic kernel RCU); writer `synchronize_rcu()` blocks ms-scale; `call_rcu()` async. Worst-case retention: unbounded under stalled readers — a CPU never quiescing blocks a grace period and every reclamation behind it.

This unbounded-retention property is shared with epoch-based reclamation but for a different reason: in EBR a stalled pinned thread freezes reclamation; in RCU a CPU that never reaches a quiescent state (e.g. a CPU stuck in a long read-side critical section) does. The kernel mitigates with preemptible RCU, expedited grace periods, and watchdog stalls.

sections.title: Userspace RCU and C++26 standardisation
sections.content:
**liburcu** (Mathieu Desnoyers) brings RCU to userspace with multiple flavours: signal-based, membarrier-based, mb (full barrier), and bp (bullet-proof). Used by QEMU, gluster, lttng, and many production systems. DPDK provides a per-data-structure RCU variant (`rte_rcu_qsbr`) with QSBR semantics; see also `qsbr`.

**C++26 `std::rcu_obj_base` / `std::rcu_synchronize`** (P2545) standardises userspace RCU as a language-level primitive alongside `std::hazard_pointer`. The C++26 form is essentially a packaging of liburcu's bp/membarrier flavours into a portable, allocator-friendly API.

Production footprint: Linux kernel uses RCU pervasively (VFS dcache, networking stack, modules, IDR/radix-tree, RCU-protected hash tables). McKenney's `Is Parallel Programming Hard, And, If So, What Can You Do About It?` is the standard reference.
